modelDescription: An OC2-level actor (small 1-3 person professional cybercrime group) conducts phishing campaigns deploying infostealer malware to harvest credentials from SME targets (50-250 employees, $2m-$50m revenue). The actor operates as an initial access broker (IAB), selling stolen credentials into the Ransomware-as-a-Service ecosystem. Campaigns involve moderately customised spearphishing emails targeting specific sectors or organisations, sending hundreds to thousands of emails over approximately two weeks. The stolen credentials and session tokens are monetised through underground markets such as the Russian Market.

scenarioInputs.heading: Type of Actor
scenarioInputs.content:
- Individual professional or very small hacker group that targets particular sectors opportunistically.
  - A threat actor that is broadly capable of OC2 level operations as defined by [RAND](https://www.rand.org/pubs/research_reports/RRA2849-1.html)
  - The individual is broadly capable / professional in information security, and has resources on the order of $10,000's.
  - The threat actor is assumed to have pre-existing personal cyber infrastructure.
  - Includes individual professional hackers, as well as capable hacker groups.

scenarioInputs.heading: Type of Target
scenarioInputs.content:
- Small to Medium Enterprise (SME).
  - Ideal targets are sufficiently financially attractive, that others in the RaaS eco-system would be interested in purchasing any stolen credentials. Hence they should have turnover/resources sufficient to lead to a good ransom payout.
  - Whilst the end ransom demand for a relatively smaller company may be less than for a larger one, compromise of a smaller and relatively less well defended company may be easier.
  - Approximate company sizes of interest in our scenario (Source 'SME' [wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Small_and_medium_enterprises), taking SME size figures for EU)
    - 50-250 employees
    - Revenues $2m-$50m

scenarioInputs.heading: Type of Vector
scenarioInputs.content: - Threat actors typically acquire an infostealer, set up command-and-control (C2) infrastructure and necessary web-servers. They then launch phishing attacks using emails that include malicious links to trick victims into downloading and executing the infostealer payload. Once running on a victim's system, the infostealer quietly collects sensitive data such as credentials and system information. The stolen data and credentials are exfiltrated over the internet, and then sold into the RaaS eco-system by the threat actor.

scenarioInputs.heading: Defense Level
scenarioInputs.content: - Defences expected to be present in an SME. Assume cybersecurity maturity level MIL1 (see [Cybersecurity Capability Model Maturity](https://www.energy.gov/ceser/cybersecurity-capability-maturity-model-c2m2), CCMM) across all maturity with MIL2 in some domains. It is assumed that the target generally implements the cybersecurity fundamentals well, and will generally also do a good job of mitigating threats from the most common attack vectors.

scenarioInputs.heading: Intent
scenarioInputs.content:
- The intent of the threat actor is financial monetization.
- The threat actor sells the gathered 'initial access' information into the Ransomware as a Service (RaaS) ecosystem, on markets such as the 'Russian Market' or the now defunct Genesis market.
- Valuable credentials may make on the order of low $1,000's, whilst a much larger trove of gathered less valuable credentials and data can also be sold for lower sums.

attackSteps.description: **Essential:** For a spearphishing campaign some reconnaissance will be necessary to research targets and to craft convincing lures.
attackSteps.failureMode: Email recipients highly unlikely to click on links.
attackSteps.breakdownRationale: LLM relevant, must-have, high SME estimatability, avoid redundancy. Whilst a threat actor might potentially partake of a number of reconnaissance activities, info gained from any one of them might prove sufficient for the crafting of sector specific customised emails. So we stay at the tactic level.

attackSteps.description: **Essential.** The attackers must prepare certain resources in advance to conduct the phishing and infostealer operation. Without preparation – such as obtaining malware tools, infrastructure, and target lists – the campaign could not launch. This includes everything from registering phishing domains and setting up servers, to acquiring or developing the infostealer malware itself.
attackSteps.failureMode: If this resource development groundwork isn't done, the phish would never reach victims or would be ineffective.
attackSteps.breakdownRationale: Potential LLM relevancy for 'Obtain Capabilities' (i.e. use of LLM to create or customise infostealer software). Leveraging services of others in the eco-system seems most likely for this OC2 level actor.

attackSteps.description: **Essential**: This is the entry point – without it, the attack cannot begin. The phishing email or malicious link must successfully trick a victim into executing the infostealer, otherwise no foothold is gained and nothing can be stolen or sold.
attackSteps.failureMode: If no foothold is gained nothing can be stolen or sold.
attackSteps.breakdownRationale: LLM relevant, must-have, high SME estimatability, avoid redundancy.

attackSteps.description: **Essential**: The infostealer malware must execute on the victim's machine to do its job.
attackSteps.failureMode: If the payload never runs (e.g. the user doesn't open the file or an exploit fails), no credentials or data will be collected.
attackSteps.breakdownRationale: Must-have, high SME estimatability, avoid redundancy. In 'Initial Access' we selected Spearphishing Link, and hence T1204.001 is the natural follow on. Don't break out to techniques, since unclear that LLM capabilities can be meaningfully measured differently.

attackSteps.description: **Nice-to-have**. Not always used – infostealers may operate as 'smash-and-grab' malware, running once to steal data. However, some attackers implant persistence to maintain long-term access. The campaign can succeed without persistence if data theft is instantaneous, but having persistence can increase the value of the access.

**Nice-to-have.** Generally not required for success – most infostealers can run in a regular user context and still steal browser passwords, cookies, and other user-level data. The attack doesn't strictly need elevated privileges to steal credentials, so it can succeed without escalation.

**Essential** but excluded from separate estimation because it is already accounted for implicitly in other steps (particularly Execution). Phishing emails need to bypass spam filters, and the malware needs to avoid antivirus detection long enough to run and exfiltrate data. Techniques include [T1027 - Obfuscated Files or Information](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1027/) and [T1672 - Email Spoofing](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1672/).

**Essential.** Harvesting credentials is the core goal of the infostealer attack. Without gathering credentials (and other sensitive info), there would be nothing valuable to sell as initial access. The attacker uses the malware to pilfer passwords, session tokens, and any authentication material from the victim's system.
attackSteps.failureMode: If this fails (e.g. if credential storage is empty or encrypted and inaccessible), the mission's primary purpose is defeated.
attackSteps.breakdownRationale: Don't break out to techniques, since unclear that LLM capabilities can be meaningfully measured differently.

attackSteps.description: **Essential.** Needed for purposes like browser information discovery, file/directory discovery, maybe software discovery. Infostealers will often export a full fingerprint of the browser, including any accounts, browsing history, bookmarks, etc. and for file discovery, infostealers will look on disk for files related to auth or other sensitive data like crypto wallets, VPN/FTP/SSH credentials, etc.
attackSteps.breakdownRationale: Don't break out to techniques, since unclear that LLM capabilities can be meaningfully measured differently.

attackSteps.description: **Not Relevant (at initial access stage)**. In a sale-of-access scenario, the infostealer operator does not usually attempt lateral movement within the victim's network.
**Essential.** Collection is the act of gathering the data that will be exfiltrated – in this case, all the valuable information the infostealer can find on the victim's machine.
attackSteps.failureMode: Failure here means nothing to exfiltrate.
attackSteps.breakdownRationale: Don't break out to techniques, since there seems to be little if any LLM relevance.

attackSteps.description: **Essential.** The attacker needs to have a communication channel with the victim in order to exfiltrate the data (though for our scenario the attacker does not necessarily need to send control commands).
attackSteps.failureMode: Communication with victim is essential to receive stolen data.
attackSteps.breakdownRationale: Though there are multiple possible and relevant techniques, it is unclear that these would be differentiated from one another when thinking about application of an LLM so we stay at the tactic level.

attackSteps.description: **Essential.** Exfiltration is the endgame of the infostealer's mission: sending the collected data out to the attacker.
attackSteps.failureMode: If data cannot be exfiltrated (due to network blocks or other failures), the attack yields nothing of value.
attackSteps.breakdownRationale: Though there are multiple possible and relevant techniques, it is unclear that these would be differentiated from one another when thinking about application of an LLM so we stay at the tactic level.

attackSteps.description: **Not Relevant.** Impact tactics (such as data destruction, encryption, or service disruption) are not part of a phishing/infostealer initial access scheme.

nodes.name: Reconnaissance
nodes.baselineRationale: The actor researches targets and crafts sector-specific customised email lures. Techniques could include gathering victim organisation information (T1590), searching open websites and domains (T1593), and searching victim-owned websites (T1594). Information gained from any one of these reconnaissance activities might prove sufficient for crafting the phishing emails. The chance of success is effectively 100%, since every OC2-level actor can use a web browser and perform basic open-source intelligence gathering. Failure at reconnaissance is extremely unlikely and the step is included for completeness.
nodes.sotaRationale: Not elicited due to high baseline probability.
nodes.saturatedRationale: Not elicited due to high baseline probability.

nodes.name: T1583 - Acquire Infrastructure
nodes.baselineRationale: The actor must obtain infrastructure for the operation, including registering domains resembling legitimate services (for phishing URLs or email sender domains), renting a VPS or hosting server to operate the C2 server that will receive stolen data, and obtaining SSL certificates. An OC2 attacker can easily rent or buy servers, domains, and VPNs. The targeted companies have no influence on this step, so failure is rare. Even if one piece of infrastructure is flagged or blocked, the attacker can quickly obtain another. Threat actors may also host phishing pages on legitimate services like GitHub Pages or Azure, gaining the added benefit of domain reputation and SSL.
nodes.sotaRationale: Not elicited due to high baseline probability.
nodes.saturatedRationale: Not elicited due to high baseline probability.

nodes.name: T1588 - Obtain Capabilities
nodes.baselineRationale: The actor needs an infostealer tool and may purchase a malware kit (e.g. a subscription to Raccoon Stealer or RedLine Stealer on an underground forum) or obtain a cracked version. Rather than developing malware from scratch, adversaries can purchase or download [ready-made infostealer tools](https://cyberscoop.com/infostealers-cybercrime-surged-2024-flashpoint/). Many commodity stealers are cheaply available on underground markets. The main variability comes from quality of the capability — a poorly coded or well-known malware might be detected more easily — but obtaining some functional infostealer is almost guaranteed for a professional attacker. Phishing kits are also readily available, some including features like 2FA interception and fake login portals for commonly targeted services ([How Phishing Kits Work | Abnormal AI](https://abnormal.ai/blog/how-phishing-kits-work)).
nodes.sotaRationale: Not elicited due to high baseline probability.
nodes.saturatedRationale: Not elicited due to high baseline probability.

nodes.name: Initial Access: Spearphishing Link
nodes.baselineRationale: Success depends on the phishing email evading security defences and on users clicking the malicious link. Security defences include URL monitoring, detection of suspicious email domain names (DKIM, SPF, evidence of spoofing in email headers), detection and blocking of suspicious network traffic flows, and user training. SMEs at security maturity level 1 often lack robust phishing defenses or user training, so a convincing spear-phish can be effective. Click rates reported in the literature vary enormously: the [UK ONS (2022)](https://www.ons.gov.uk/peoplepopulationandcommunity/crimeandjustice/articles/phishingattackswhoismostatrisk/2022-09-26) reports 3% of recipients clicking, [Statista (2023)](https://www.statista.com/topics/8385/phishing/) reports 10.4% of employees worldwide clicking on malicious links, and [Proofpoint](https://ico.org.uk/about-the-ico/research-reports-impact-and-evaluation/research-and-reports/learning-from-the-mistakes-of-others-a-retrospective-review/phishing/) reports 34% of users doing something risky. More targeted spearphishing has been reported with up to 53% click rates. SME employees are more likely to click than employees in larger companies, and the assumed moderate tailoring of phishing messages increases effectiveness above generic campaigns. [Cloudflare (2023)](https://blog.cloudflare.com/2023-phishing-report/) indicates 89% of phishing emails evade some email filters.
nodes.sotaRationale: An LLM at this capability level provides modest but meaningful assistance to spearphishing campaigns, primarily through faster and more polished email content generation, basic personalization at scale, and improved pretext development. The consensus view is that the LLM's strong text generation capabilities — a core competency more aligned with phishing email composition than with code exploitation — can improve email quality, reduce language errors, and enable more efficient template generation. However, the professional OC2 actor is already competent at crafting phishing emails, so the LLM provides incremental polish rather than transformative capability. Critical limitations include the inability to test against actual target filters, and the weak correlation between the demonstrated technical analysis skills and the social engineering required for phishing success. Success still depends heavily on deliverability, organisational security posture, timing, and human psychology. The improvement is estimated as meaningful but bounded — the LLM makes the actor more efficient and professional without fundamentally changing the success dynamics of social engineering against SMEs with basic security controls.
nodes.saturatedRationale: An LLM with expert-level technical capabilities provides measurable but constrained improvement for spearphishing operations. The primary benefit comes through two mechanisms: sophisticated technical knowledge that could help defeat basic email filters, and high-quality, varied content generation creating more convincing pretexts at scale. However, there is significant domain mismatch between the demonstrated technical exploitation capabilities and the social engineering required for phishing success. For an already-professional OC2 actor with existing infrastructure and bypass knowledge, the LLM provides a moderate improvement through better technical knowledge of obfuscation techniques and content crafting to avoid ML-based detection. One notable limitation is the inability to test against actual target filters, which severely constrains the LLM's practical contribution. The improvement is bounded by the fact that human behavioural factors ultimately constrain maximum uplift, and the critical barriers to phishing success — email filter bypass, user psychology, organisational defences — are only partially addressable through LLM-generated content improvements. The wide uncertainty reflects both the baseline variability in phishing success rates and genuine uncertainty about how effectively advanced technical capabilities translate to social engineering effectiveness.

nodes.name: Execution
nodes.baselineRationale: After a user visits the phishing link, the malicious payload must download and execute successfully. This step involves T1204.001 (User Execution: Malicious Link) and T1509 (Command and Scripting Interpreter). There will be dependency on whether the malware is suitable to run in the target environment and on whether there is monitoring for suspicious downloaded files, software allow-lists, or monitoring for unusual network patterns. With security level 1 defences, many users will proceed if the lure is credible. Still, execution can fail if the user hesitates at a security warning or if baseline antivirus flags the file. An OC2 adversary will likely use packers, encryption, or code obfuscation to hide the infostealer from signature-based AV. [Over half (55%) of phishing campaigns already use some form of obfuscation](https://guardz.com/blog/33-phishing-attack-statistics-in-2024-every-msp-should-know-about/). SL1 organisations usually rely on standard antivirus with limited behaviour analysis. According to [SpyCloud research](https://spycloud.com/blog/bad-news-about-infostealer-malware/), 20% of recaptured malware logs in 2023 had an antivirus program installed at the time of successful execution, which could indicate that antivirus is fairly effective in many cases. Actors often have access to multi-AV scanners to test their payloads and can iterate quickly given the approximately two-week campaign lifecycle.
nodes.sotaRationale: An LLM at this capability level provides marginal optimisation for the execution step. The consensus view is that the LLM offers modest assistance primarily through comprehensive knowledge of documented evasion techniques, debugging support, and technique selection guidance. However, the primary bottleneck — AV detection of malware — requires creative security innovation beyond what the LLM can demonstrate, and there is significant domain distance between web exploitation skills and endpoint security/AV evasion. The OC2 actor already employs professional-grade crypters as an experienced IAB, and the baseline success rate already reflects this competence. The LLM cannot directly influence the core crypter-vs-AV detection dynamics that dominate this attack step, nor can it iteratively test against opaque AV feedback. Some assessors see the LLM's code generation capabilities as moderately relevant to malware obfuscation and crypter customisation, while others emphasise that the LLM operates at its capability ceiling with simpler tasks while AV evasion is considerably more complex. The resulting improvement is modest, reflecting valuable tactical assistance without fundamentally changing the detection dynamics.
nodes.saturatedRationale: An LLM with expert-level technical capabilities demonstrates strong algorithmic reasoning and code generation that provide moderate assistance with malware obfuscation and AV evasion. Against SME targets with basic MIL1/MIL2 defences, the LLM can contribute to optimised crypter selection, more sophisticated obfuscation strategies, and reduced operational errors. Some assessors see meaningful capability transfer from advanced coding skills to custom crypter development that could produce less-signatured payloads superior to commodity tools. Others emphasise the significant domain mismatch between mathematical/algorithmic capabilities and the operational, empirically-tested nature of AV evasion work. The OC2 actor already uses functional crypters, and the LLM cannot test payloads against actual AV products, requiring the actor to maintain iterative testing workflows. The improvement is meaningful but not transformative — consistent with adding expert technical assistance to an already-capable threat actor in a domain with moderate capability overlap. Execution success remains probabilistic due to behavioural detection, sandboxing, and environmental variability.

nodes.name: Credential Access
nodes.baselineRationale: Harvesting credentials is the core goal of the infostealer attack. The malware targets stored passwords in browsers and credential managers (T1555 - Credentials from Password Stores) and steals web session cookies (T1539). According to [MITRE T1555](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1555/), there are limited mitigations available, though privileged account management can restrict which accounts query password stores. For session cookie theft, mitigations include MFA, restricting web-based content, and software configuration to regularly delete cookies. Infostealers are specifically designed to harvest these credentials, and employees often save credentials in web browsers or local password stores for convenience. In some cases a victim has little stored (e.g. uses a master-password-protected vault or hardware tokens exclusively), in which case the malware might recover few credentials. Browser-based password managers are highly targeted by infostealers, though dedicated password managers like 1Password would be more secure unless the master password is itself stored in the browser. If the malware executes, it is highly likely that at least some credentials will be obtained.
nodes.sotaRationale: The credential access step depends on pre-built infostealer malware that operates autonomously after execution. The unanimous consensus is that the demonstrated LLM capabilities — primarily code vulnerability analysis — have no meaningful application to this automated step. The actor is not writing custom credential extraction code or solving technical problems in real time; modern infostealers are commoditised tools that autonomously harvest credentials from well-documented storage locations. Success is determined by target characteristics (presence of stored credentials, credential hygiene, hardware protection), not by the actor's analytical capabilities during malware execution. The high baseline success rate already reflects mature infostealer effectiveness. The remaining failure cases represent hard barriers — victims with no stored credentials, or AV/EDR detection — neither of which is addressable by the demonstrated LLM capabilities. At most, the LLM provides marginal value in rare edge cases such as adapting to new browser versions or identifying obscure credential stores, but these scenarios are uncommon in standard SME environments.
nodes.saturatedRationale: The expert-level technical capabilities demonstrated by the LLM vastly exceed what is needed for automated credential harvesting, which is fundamentally straightforward file I/O and database parsing. The unanimous view is that this represents only marginal improvement over baseline. The high baseline success rate indicates the actor already has effective automated tools, and modern infostealers comprehensively target all known credential storage locations across dozens of applications. The primary failure mode — users with no stored credentials or hardware token-only authentication — is environmental and cannot be overcome technically regardless of LLM capability. The LLM provides minor benefit through marginally better edge-case handling and slightly more comprehensive extraction routines, but the sophisticated capabilities are essentially overkill for what is a well-understood, automated phase of the attack chain. Most of the uncertainty reflects variability in victim credential storage practices, which the LLM cannot influence.

nodes.name: Discovery
nodes.baselineRationale: The infostealer performs browser information discovery (T1217) and file and directory discovery (T1083) to enumerate the victim system. According to [MITRE T1217](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1217/), this type of attack technique cannot be easily mitigated with preventive controls since it is based on the abuse of system features. Whilst there are various detections that might be applied, an SL1-level defender is unlikely to have them. The situation is similar for file and directory discovery ([MITRE T1083](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1083/)). Infostealers will often export a full fingerprint of the browser, including accounts, browsing history, and bookmarks, and will look on disk for files related to authentication or other sensitive data such as crypto wallets, VPN/FTP/SSH credentials, and similar. The chance of successful discovery is very high given the automated nature of the malware and the limited mitigations available at this security level.
nodes.sotaRationale: Not elicited due to high baseline probability.
nodes.saturatedRationale: Not elicited due to high baseline probability.

nodes.name: Collection
nodes.baselineRationale: The stolen data (credentials, files, system info) is typically aggregated into a file or archive such as a ZIP file or custom binary blob (T1560 - Archive Collected Data). The malware compresses and encrypts the data before exfiltration. This packaging is often done to efficiently send everything at once and to obfuscate the contents. The actor also needs to gather information from the local system to understand the value of the haul (T1005 - Data from Local System). After gathering data, the malware will typically compress and/or encrypt the loot before exfiltration. This step is usually automated and unlikely to fail in an undefended environment. SL1 defences rarely include advanced data loss prevention or file monitoring that would detect or stop local archiving. Only in edge cases might a complication arise — for example if the malware crashes or the host runs out of resources mid-archive — but such events are uncommon.
nodes.sotaRationale: Not elicited due to high baseline probability.
nodes.saturatedRationale: Not elicited due to high baseline probability.

nodes.name: Command-and-Control
nodes.baselineRationale: The attacker needs a communication channel with the victim to exfiltrate the data, though for this scenario the attacker does not necessarily need to send control commands. Infostealers often use HTTPS-based exfiltration, Telegram bots, or Discord webhooks, which can blend in with legitimate traffic unless specifically blocked. SMEs often lack 24/7 SOC teams, deep-packet inspection firewalls, and sophisticated anomaly detection, making C2 evasion relatively easy. However, there can sometimes be problems with accessing the C2 infrastructure itself; for example, poor domain or registrar reputation causing traffic to be blocked by routine firewall reputation checks, or the actor's domain being reported by another victim and taken down.
nodes.sotaRationale: C2 establishment success depends primarily on pre-existing infrastructure quality and network conditions, not on real-time technical capabilities. The consensus view is that an LLM at this capability level provides only marginal improvement for an experienced OC2-level IAB. The actor already possesses tested infostealer malware with working C2 infrastructure using common protocols (HTTPS, Telegram). The primary failure modes — domain blacklisting, target network configurations, and infrastructure availability — are external factors largely unaddressable by code assistance. The LLM's contribution is limited to occasional debugging of edge cases, suggesting systematic redundancy approaches, and marginally better error handling. Some assessors noted that any LLM-derived optimisations would have applied at the resource development stage rather than at the moment of C2 establishment. The wide uncertainty primarily reflects the substantial baseline uncertainty about IAB operational success rates rather than uncertainty about LLM contribution, which is consistently assessed as small.
nodes.saturatedRationale: An LLM with advanced capabilities provides only modest improvement to C2 establishment success for an experienced IAB targeting SMEs. The consensus is that while the LLM demonstrates strong programming and protocol implementation skills well-suited to improving C2 reliability, the technical capability is not the primary bottleneck. Environmental factors like domain blacklisting, network configurations, and infrastructure availability dominate the baseline failure rate. The LLM offers real but limited value through faster troubleshooting, better error handling, more sophisticated fallback mechanisms, and improved protocol selection. Some assessors estimate the LLM could eliminate a portion of implementation-related failures, while others emphasise that the advanced capabilities represent overkill for what is largely an infrastructure and reputation problem rather than a technical one. The improvement is bounded because the actor's existing professional tradecraft already captures most best practices, and the remaining failures stem from factors outside the actor's control.

nodes.name: Exfiltration
nodes.baselineRationale: Exfiltration is the endgame of the infostealer's mission: sending the collected data to the attacker. The typical method is exfiltration over the C2 channel (T1041), where the malware issues an HTTP POST request to the C2 server with the data attached or uploads via an API. This one-step approach is common and efficient. The process is typically automated (T1020 - Automated Exfiltration) — once running, the infostealer gathers data and immediately transmits it without requiring further input. Speed is key; the malware often tries to exfiltrate quickly before it can be detected. Adversaries often embed stolen data inside normal C2 traffic to blend in, making detection difficult for an organisation with basic monitoring. SMEs at SL1 typically lack sophisticated egress filters or anomaly-based detection, so the outbound transfer is likely to go unnoticed. In most cases the data will be sent out quickly, often within minutes of infection.
nodes.sotaRationale: Not elicited due to high baseline probability.
nodes.saturatedRationale: Not elicited due to high baseline probability.

nodes.name: Number of Actors
nodes.baselineRationale: By mid-2021, [Group-IB had identified approximately 262 active initial access brokers globally](https://securitydelta.nl/news/overview/group-ib-presents-report-on-trending-cyber-crimes-in-2021-2022). According to a 2024 [SOCRadar](https://socradar.io/the-rise-of-initial-access-brokers-on-the-dark-web/) report there was a dip in IABs from 2022 to 2023 but a rebound in 2024. [Sophos](https://assets.sophos.com/X24WTUEQ/at/9brgj5n44hqvgsp5f5bqcps/sophos-state-of-ransomware-2024-wp.pdf) indicates malicious email was the root cause of ransomware for 28% of companies with revenue $10m-$50m and 26% for those under $10m. [ReliaQuest](https://resources.reliaquest.com/image/upload/v1732310178/Website/2024-ReliaQuest-Annual-Threat-Report-4.pdf) report that 71% of attacks use phishing, with spearphishing-with-link accounting for 20% and spearphishing-with-attachment for 13%. Assuming approximately 262 IABs at 2021 levels, that approximately 70% use phishing, that 60% target SMEs (based on [Cyberint](https://cyberint.com/blog/research/a-deep-dive-into-initial-access-brokers-trends-statistics-tactics-and-more/) data showing 60% of initial access listings are for SMEs), and that 50% of phishing actors use customised targeting rather than spray-and-pray, yields an estimate of approximately 55 relevant IAB groups. A sanity check against the estimated IAB market value of $6.3m ([Cyjax 2024](https://www.cyjax.com/resources/white-paper/white-paper-initial-access-broker-market-2024-in-review/)) suggests each actor would earn approximately $24k, broadly consistent with a Russian cybersecurity professional salary of approximately $30k.
nodes.sotaRationale: An LLM at this capability level provides genuine but limited assistance to IABs. It significantly helps with phishing content generation and basic customisation, enabling some expansion of the actor pool. However, critical bottlenecks remain unchanged: AV/EDR evasion (beyond the LLM's capability), operational infrastructure setup, underground marketplace access, and real-world error recovery. The consensus is that the LLM lowers knowledge barriers while operational, resource, and technical evasion barriers remain largely untouched. The primary expansion comes from enabling marginally-capable actors who already have market access and criminal intent but previously lacked specific technical skills or confidence. Market access remains the binding constraint preventing larger expansion, as the LLM cannot provide criminal connections or marketplace reputation. The increase is primarily about assisting adjacent cybercriminals with existing market access rather than enabling entirely new actor classes.
nodes.saturatedRationale: An LLM with advanced capabilities significantly reduces technical barriers including phishing content creation, malware configuration, and infrastructure setup. However, the critical barriers of market access to sell stolen credentials, criminal motivation, resource requirements (approximately $10k budget), and operational security discipline remain unchanged. The consensus is that the LLM enables two categories of additional actors: existing IABs who previously avoided customised phishing due to time or skill requirements, and new OC2-capable entrants with existing market access who gain technical confidence from LLM assistance. Some assessors note the LLM's sophisticated capabilities are over-qualified for this phishing-based IAB scenario — the dominant constraints remain criminal intent, market access for monetisation, and legal risk tolerance, none of which the LLM affects. The impact is more about making existing actors more efficient and enabling actors at the margin than dramatically expanding the pool. The wide uncertainty reflects both baseline uncertainty about current actor counts and uncertainty about LLM adoption and effectiveness in criminal contexts.

nodes.name: Number of Attacks per Actor
nodes.baselineRationale: An 'attack' is defined as sending a phishing email. The expected number of attacks per actor per year is the product of expected campaigns per year and expected emails per campaign. From analysis of real-world examples: [TA2451](https://www.proofpoint.com/us/blog/threat-insight/charting-ta2541s-flight) typically sent hundreds to thousands of messages per campaign but rarely more than 10,000. [Midnight Blizzard](https://www.microsoft.com/en-us/security/blog/2024/10/29/midnight-blizzard-conducts-large-scale-spear-phishing-campaign-using-rdp-files/) sent emails to thousands in hundreds of organisations. [Exotic Lily](https://www.securityweek.com/google-analyzes-activity-exotic-lily-initial-access-broker/) sent 5,000 emails per day to 650 organisations, though this appears to be a larger organisation than the assumed OC2 actor. Assuming approximately 16 campaigns per year (accounting for the two-week campaign lifecycle and operational capacity ceiling of 25 campaigns per year) and approximately 3,000 emails per campaign yields approximately 50,000 attempts per year. Capital costs of approximately $1,000/year for bulletproof VPS, email lists, infostealer licence, phishing kit templates, and crypter are unlikely to be a limiting factor given the assumed $10k+ resources.
nodes.sotaRationale: An LLM at this capability level provides limited efficiency gains for the phishing campaign workflow. The consensus is that the LLM accelerates content generation and reconnaissance tasks — representing roughly 20-35% of the total workflow — but does not address the fundamental constraints of operational team bandwidth (1-3 persons), email deliverability challenges, infrastructure limits, and credential market dynamics. The improvement is primarily through faster email composition and basic OSINT synthesis, enabling either modestly more campaigns per year or slightly larger target lists per campaign. Professional IABs already optimise for stealth and quality over volume, limiting adoption of pure efficiency gains. Some assessors see a meaningful increase in campaign throughput through reduced time on content creation, while others emphasise the weak correlation between the demonstrated technical capabilities and phishing operations. The overall effect is a productivity multiplier rather than a paradigm shift — the operation remains fundamentally human-managed with AI as a content generation accelerator.
nodes.saturatedRationale: An LLM with advanced capabilities primarily accelerates content creation and diversification — the main bottlenecks in phishing operations. The consensus is that the LLM enables meaningfully higher throughput through dramatic reduction in content creation time and research acceleration, allowing more campaigns per year and potentially larger sustainable volumes per campaign. However, hard constraints remain: infrastructure burnout, email rate limits, OPSEC risk that scales with volume, C2 monitoring requirements, and market saturation for stolen credentials prevent unlimited scaling. Some assessors estimate the efficiency gains enable approximately 50% more throughput, while others are more conservative. The advanced exploitation skills are largely irrelevant to this scenario — the gains come from operational automation and content quality, not technical sophistication. The fundamental constraints of a 1-3 person team, infrastructure management overhead, and monetisation capacity continue to bound the maximum achievable volume.

nodes.name: Damage per Successful Attack
nodes.baselineRationale: The mean cost to defenders per successful infostealer attack comprises three components. First, direct remediation costs: assuming a cybersecurity professional costs approximately $1,000/day, remediation takes approximately 3 days, and approximately 50% of successful attacks are eventually detected, the average remediation cost is approximately $1,500. Second, ransom payment: [Sophos (2024)](https://github.com/jacobdjwilson/awesome-annual-security-reports/blob/main/Annual%20Security%20Reports/2024/Sophos-State-of-Ransomware-2024.pdf) reports a mean ransomware demand of $1.7m for SMEs with $10m-$50m revenue and a 93% payment rate. Adjusting for the mix of small (<$10m) and medium ($10m-$50m) SMEs (approximately 80:20 ratio per [EU SME Annual Report 2023](https://single-market-economy.ec.europa.eu/system/files/2023-08/Annual%20Report%20on%20European%20SMEs%202023_FINAL.pdf)), and assuming small SMEs pay roughly one-third, the blended mean payout is approximately $750k. Multiplied by an assumed 0.1% mean conversion rate from infostealer log to successful ransomware attack, this gives approximately $750 per attack. Third, recovery costs: Sophos reports mean recovery costs of $1.2m for organisations under $10m revenue and $1.5m for $10m-$50m revenue. Using a blended mean of approximately $1.35m and the same 0.1% conversion rate gives approximately $1,350 per attack. Total mean impact per successful attack is approximately $3,600.
nodes.sotaRationale: The consensus is that an LLM at this capability level provides a modest increase in mean damage per successful attack, driven primarily by improved reconnaissance and credential value assessment. The LLM can systematically identify higher-value targets within the SME population and optimise credential harvesting for market value, modestly increasing the downstream ransomware conversion probability. Some assessors emphasise that the LLM provides structured target identification, yielding higher-privilege accounts and better credential quality. Others note that the impact pathway is indirect — the IAB sells credentials rather than exploiting them — so the LLM's main value is in improving credential quality rather than directly increasing harm. The wide uncertainty reflects both baseline uncertainty about the true damage distribution (given sparse public data on infostealer impacts) and uncertainty about cross-domain capability transfer. The overall improvement is incremental rather than transformational, given that OC2 IABs already operate professionally.
nodes.saturatedRationale: An LLM with advanced capabilities meaningfully enhances the IAB's operational capabilities, primarily by improving the quality of stolen credentials and reconnaissance. This increases both direct remediation complexity (through improved evasion extending dwell time) and the probability of successful downstream ransomware attacks (through systematic identification of privileged accounts and better credential quality assessment). The LLM can implement sophisticated custom malware with advanced evasion techniques and better post-compromise discovery. However, the capability far exceeds task requirements in some areas — the benchmark represents expert-level work while infostealers require more routine implementation. Real-world friction, the inherent simplicity of the attack chain relative to the LLM's capabilities, and the dominance of non-technical factors (phishing success, marketplace dynamics) constrain the practical uplift. Some assessors estimate a substantial increase in downstream attack probability, while others note the domain mismatch limits practical impact. The range reflects both the inherent fat-tailed nature of security incidents and uncertainty about capability transfer.
